critic_raw:
This RNN vs. logistic regression hospital readmission experiment contains multiple serious flaws. Most critically, the test set policy in Section 6 explicitly states: 'after selecting hyperparameters using the validation set, a final check on a subset of the test set is performed to ensure the chosen model configuration has an advantage before committing to full training. If the test subset indicates validation overfitting, slight adjustments are made before full test evaluation.' This is unambiguous test set contamination: the test set drives model adjustment, making the final test evaluation optimistically biased and scientifically invalid.

Second, the split strategy uses stratified random sampling across all 5 years — not a temporal split. Patient readmission involves temporal dynamics: coding practices, treatment protocols, and discharge criteria change over 5 years. A random split allows the model to train on 2024 patient data and test on 2020 patient data, in effect seeing future clinical patterns during training. This invalidates the evaluation for deployment scenarios.

Third, the preprocessing pipeline (imputation, scaling, encoding) is 'fit on the entire dataset prior to splitting.' This is a data leakage error compounding the split strategy problem.

Fourth, the data selection criterion ('primarily from patients with a history of frequent healthcare utilization') creates a systematic selection bias. High-utilization patients have structurally higher readmission rates and more complete EHR records. A model trained on this subpopulation will not generalize to the broader inpatient population, and the hypothesis framing ('particularly for high-risk patients') does not justify this as the target population.

Finally, macro-averaged F1 as the primary metric in a severely imbalanced setting (10-15% readmission rate) will be dominated by the majority class performance, contradicting the stated goal of 'minimizing false negatives among high-risk patients.'
all_issues_raised: Explicit test set contamination: test subset is used to verify model advantage and trigger 'slight adjustments' before final evaluation — this is a direct violation of held-out evaluation integrity
Random split in a temporally structured dataset: 5-year patient data split randomly allows future clinical patterns to appear in training, invalidating deployment-relevant generalization
Data leakage: preprocessing (imputation, scaling, encoding) fitted on entire dataset before splitting
Selection bias: restricting to 'frequent healthcare utilization' patients creates a non-representative high-risk subpopulation that does not generalize to the broader inpatient population
Macro-averaged F1 in a 10-15% imbalanced setting is dominated by the majority class, contradicting the stated clinical goal of minimizing false negatives for high-risk patients

Critical test set contamination: a subset of the test set is explicitly consulted before full test evaluation, with parameter adjustments permitted if validation overfitting is detected — this directly invalidates the holdout
Stratified random split over 5 years creates temporal leakage by mixing future patients into the training set, inconsistent with deployment scenarios where the model must generalize forward in time

Preprocessing data leakage: imputation, scaling, and encoding statistics are fit on the full dataset before splitting, incorporating test-period information into training normalization — with an incorrect justification given the large sample size

Dataset restricted to high-utilization patients introduces selection bias, making findings non-representative of the broader readmission-relevant population and potentially inflating model performance

The LSTM's early stopping uses validation F1-score while logistic regression uses grid search over a regularization parameter on the validation set — asymmetric tuning procedures despite the claim of equivalent effort

Random encounter-level split for longitudinal patient data creates temporal leakage — same patient's earlier encounters can appear in test set while later encounters (including future readmissions) are in training
Preprocessing transformers explicitly fit on entire dataset before splitting — imputation statistics and scaling parameters from validation and test data contaminate training preprocessing

Explicit test set peek with post-peek adjustment: design admits 'slight adjustments are made before full test evaluation' based on a test subset check, completely invalidating the test set as an unbiased performance estimate
Macro F1 as primary metric for 10-15% minority class gives equal weight to majority class (non-readmission) performance, which is not clinically meaningful for the stated stakeholder objective

Data selection biases toward 'patients with a history of frequent healthcare utilization' — this non-representative sampling creates a model trained on a high-utilization subpopulation that may not generalize to first-time or low-utilization patients
